title: Candy
questionBody:
There are n children standing in a line. Each child is assigned a rating value given in the integer array ratings.

You are giving candies to these children subjected to the following requirements:

  • Each child must have at least one candy.
  • Children with a higher rating get more candies than their neighbors.

Return the minimum number of candies you need to have to distribute the candies to the children.

 

Example 1:

Input: ratings = [1,0,2]
Output: 5
Explanation: You can allocate to the first, second and third child with 2, 1, 2 candies respectively.

Example 2:

Input: ratings = [1,2,2]
Output: 4
Explanation: You can allocate to the first, second and third child with 1, 2, 1 candies respectively.
The third child gets 1 candy because it satisfies the above two conditions.

 

Constraints:

  • n == ratings.length
  • 1 <= n <= 2 * 104
  • 0 <= ratings[i] <= 2 * 104
clarifyingQuestions: What are the possible ranges for the integer values in the `ratings` array? Can they be negative, zero, or very large?
What is the expected behavior if the input `ratings` array is empty or contains only one element?
If two adjacent children have the same rating, do they need to receive the same number of candies, or can they have different amounts as long as they each have at least one?
Is there an upper limit on the size of the `ratings` array? I want to understand potential memory constraints.
Are we looking for the absolute minimum number of candies, or is there a tolerance for a slightly sub-optimal solution if it simplifies the implementation?

edgeCases.handling: Return 0 because there are no children to distribute candies to.
Return 1 since only one candy is needed.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Array with two children, ratings are equal
edgeCases.handling: Return 2, each child gets one candy.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Array with two children, one rating higher than other
edgeCases.handling: Return 3; one child gets 1 candy, other gets 2.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Array with all children having the same rating
edgeCases.handling: Each child receives exactly one candy, return the array's length.
The candies should increment from 1 to n, so the sum of integers from 1 to n will be the result.
The candies should decrement from n to 1, so the sum of integers from 1 to n will be the result.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Array with a V-shaped rating pattern (one child is lowest rated)
edgeCases.handling: The algorithm correctly handles this, since it iterates both left to right and right to left.

bruteForce.overview: The brute force approach to the candy problem involves trying every single possible candy assignment. We start by giving each child the minimum amount of candy and then methodically explore every possible way to adjust those amounts.
steps: Begin by giving each child one candy to start.
Check if the candy distribution is 'fair' by making sure children with higher ratings get more candy than their neighbors with lower ratings.
If the distribution is not fair, systematically increase the candies given to each child one by one, checking after each adjustment if the distribution has become fair.
Repeat this process of adjusting the number of candies and checking for fairness until all possible combinations of candy assignments have been tried.
Of all the candy distributions that are considered 'fair', choose the one that uses the least amount of total candy.

bigO.runtimeExplanation: The provided brute force approach attempts every possible candy assignment until it finds a fair distribution. Because there is no limit on the number of candies a child can receive, and we check every combination, the algorithm will continue indefinitely if a 'fair' distribution that is also the minimal one is never reached or the algorithm has no effective stopping condition. Therefore, the time complexity approaches infinity, as it might never terminate. In a practical sense, without imposed constraints, the algorithm could theoretically run forever.
bigO.spaceExplanation: The brute force approach described primarily involves iteratively adjusting candy counts in place and checking for fairness. It doesn't appear to use any auxiliary data structures like lists, hashmaps, or recursion. The algorithm only requires a few constant space variables, such as counters to track the current candy distribution being explored and a variable to store the minimum fair candy sum found so far. Therefore, the space complexity is O(1) because the memory usage remains constant regardless of the input size, N (the number of children).

code.Ruby:
def candy_brute_force(ratings)
  number_of_children = ratings.length
  minimum_candies = Float::INFINITY

  # Iterate through all possible candy assignments.
  (1..10).each do |first_candy|
    (1..10).each do |second_candy|
      if number_of_children > 2
        (1..10).each do |third_candy|
          if number_of_children > 3
            (1..10).each do |fourth_candy|
              candy_assignment = [first_candy, second_candy, third_candy, fourth_candy]
              if number_of_children > 4
                (1..10).each do |fifth_candy|
                  candy_assignment = [first_candy, second_candy, third_candy, fourth_candy, fifth_candy]
                end
              else
                candy_assignment = [first_candy, second_candy, third_candy, fourth_candy]
              end
            end
          else
            candy_assignment = [first_candy, second_candy, third_candy]
          end
        end
      else
        candy_assignment = [first_candy, second_candy]
      end

      next unless candy_assignment.length == number_of_children

      is_fair = true
      (0...number_of_children).each do |index|
        # Check left neighbor.
        if index > 0 && ratings[index] > ratings[index - 1] && candy_assignment[index] <= candy_assignment[index - 1]
          is_fair = false
          break
        end

        # Check right neighbor.
        if index < number_of_children - 1 && ratings[index] > ratings[index + 1] && candy_assignment[index] <= candy_assignment[index + 1]
          is_fair = false
          break
        end
      end

      # Only consider fair distributions.
      if is_fair
        total_candies = candy_assignment.sum
        # Track the minimum number of candies required
        minimum_candies = [minimum_candies, total_candies].min
      end
    end
  end

  # Handle cases where no solution is found
  if minimum_candies == Float::INFINITY
    return 0
  else
    return minimum_candies
  end
end
code.PHP:
<?php

function candyBruteForce(array $ratings): int
{
    $numberChildren = count($ratings);
    $minimumCandies = PHP_INT_MAX;

    function isFairDistribution(array $candies, array $ratings): bool
    {
        $numberChildren = count($ratings);
        for ($index = 0; $index < $numberChildren; $index++) {
            // Check left neighbor
            if ($index > 0 && $ratings[$index] > $ratings[$index - 1] && $candies[$index] <= $candies[$index - 1]) {
                return false;
            }
            // Check right neighbor
            if ($index < $numberChildren - 1 && $ratings[$index] > $ratings[$index + 1] && $candies[$index] <= $candies[$index + 1]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }

    function calculateTotalCandies(array $candies): int
    {
        return array_sum($candies);
    }

    // Iterate through all possible candy distributions.
    for ($i = 0; $i < pow(5, $numberChildren); $i++) {
        $candies = array_fill(0, $numberChildren, 1);
        $temp = $i;

        for ($childIndex = 0; $childIndex < $numberChildren; $childIndex++) {
            $candies[$childIndex] += $temp % 5;
            $temp = intdiv($temp, 5);
        }

        // Verify that the current assignment meets the problem criteria
        if (isFairDistribution($candies, $ratings)) {

            $totalCandies = calculateTotalCandies($candies);

            // We are finding minimum so store total candies
            $minimumCandies = min($minimumCandies, $totalCandies);
        }
    }

    return $minimumCandies;
}

optimal.overview: This problem asks us to fairly distribute candies to children based on their ratings. The key insight is to ensure each child receives at least one candy and that children with higher ratings get more candies than their immediate neighbors. We can achieve this efficiently by traversing the line of children twice: once from left to right and then from right to left, making local adjustments to the candy distribution.
steps: Give every child one candy to start.
Go through the children from left to right. If a child has a higher rating than the child to their left, give them one more candy than the child to their left.

Now, go through the children from right to left. If a child has a higher rating than the child to their right, make sure they have more candies than the child to their right. But be careful: if they already have enough candies from the first pass, don't reduce the candies they already have.
Add up the number of candies each child has. This is the minimum number of candies needed to satisfy the problem's rules.

bigO.runtimeExplanation: The algorithm iterates through the array of children's ratings twice. The first loop traverses from left to right to adjust candy counts based on the left neighbor's rating. The second loop goes from right to left, adjusting candy counts based on the right neighbor's rating while ensuring we keep the maximum candies assigned. Both loops visit each of the n children once. Therefore, the time complexity is proportional to the number of children, resulting in O(n).
bigO.spaceExplanation: The algorithm uses an auxiliary array called 'candies' to store the number of candies allocated to each child. This array has the same length as the input array 'ratings', where N represents the number of children (and the length of the ratings array). Therefore, the space required by the 'candies' array grows linearly with the input size N. No other significant auxiliary data structures are used.

code.Ruby:
def candy(ratings)
  number_of_children = ratings.length
  candies = Array.new(number_of_children, 1)

  # Ensure left neighbor gets less candy if they have lower rating
  (1...number_of_children).each do |i|
    if ratings[i] > ratings[i - 1]
      candies[i] = candies[i - 1] + 1
    end
  end

  # Ensure right neighbor gets less candy if they have lower rating
  (number_of_children - 2).downto(0).each do |i|
    if ratings[i] > ratings[i + 1]
      candies[i] = [candies[i], candies[i + 1] + 1].max
    end
  end

  # Sum up all the candies that each child has.
  total_candies = 0
  candies.each { |candy_count| total_candies += candy_count }

  return total_candies
end
code.PHP:
<?php
function candy(array $ratings): int {
    $number_of_children = count($ratings);

    #
    $candies = array_fill(0, $number_of_children, 1);

    # We go left to right, ensuring each child gets at least one candy.
    for ($i = 1; $i < $number_of_children; $i++) {
        if ($ratings[$i] > $ratings[$i - 1]) {
            $candies[$i] = $candies[$i - 1] + 1;
        }
    }

    # This pass ensures higher rated children get more candies.
    for ($i = $number_of_children - 2; $i >= 0; $i--) {
        if ($ratings[$i] > $ratings[$i + 1]) {
            #
            $candies[$i] = max($candies[$i], $candies[$i + 1] + 1);
        }
    }

    $total_candies = 0;

    # Aggregate the candies handed out
    for ($i = 0; $i < $number_of_children; $i++) {
        $total_candies += $candies[$i];
    }

    return $total_candies;
}
?>
